summary: Cathy O'Neil introduces the concept of 'big data dark matter' - the vast, hidden ecosystem of algorithmic models that secretly filter and score people without their knowledge. She illustrates this with a teacher screening algorithm being considered by Madison schools and connects it to broader concerns about health data from fitness trackers being exploited in regulatory gray areas. The post argues that these secret models are proliferating rapidly due to the explosion of personal data, and that most people have no idea they're being evaluated by black-box systems. O'Neil calls for crowdsourced documentation of these hidden algorithms.
key_points: A company sells pre-VAM teacher screening algorithms that filter job applicants before humans ever see their applications
Fitness tracker data exists in a regulatory gray area where health privacy laws don't apply, making it ripe for exploitation by employers and insurers
The secrecy of proprietary models combined with their secret deployment means the public has no way to know they're being scored
This dark matter of big data is only occasionally visible through local journalism or insider leaks
The problem isn't new (targeting subprime customers for fees existed decades ago) but has exploded with social media and personal data proliferation
money_quotes: It's just one example of the dark matter of big data.
The vast majority of big data dark matter is still there in the shadows. You can only guess at its existence and its usage.
Since the models themselves are proprietary, and are generally deployed secretly, there's no reason for the public to be informed.
The key is that phrase 'regulatory gray area'; it should make you think 'big data dark matter lives here'.
When you have unprotected data that can be used as a proxy of HIPAA-protected medical data, there's no reason it won't be.
Cold comfort for people who got removed by some random black box algorithm.
Her job was to model which customers to offer subprime credit cards to, and she was specifically told to target those customers who would end up paying the most in fees.
key_insight: There exists a vast shadow ecosystem of secret algorithmic scoring systems that evaluate people without their knowledge, and we can only detect them through accidental disclosures like local budget meetings or insider tips.
